patches.rationale: WIN-045 incorrectly cross-references Section 4.5.4 (Solar Diameter). The tidal pattern self-contradiction is in Section 4.5.2 ('Tidal Pattern: One Spike vs. Two Bulges'). Fix cross-reference to match.
WIN-049 incorrectly cross-references Section 4.5.4. The relevant self-contradiction (tidal pattern) is in Section 4.5.2. Same fix as WIN-045.

patches.find: tidal force varies by only ~6.6% across Earth's diameter
patches.replace: tidal force varies by only ~6.6% across Earth's diameter (first-order approximation; the exact near-far ratio is ~10%)
patches.rationale: The 6.6% figure is the first-order Taylor approximation (4d/R). The exact near-far force ratio is ~10%. Both values make the argument (still vastly smaller than dome's 99.4% drop), but noting both is more accurate and preempts a dome defender's precision objection.

patches.find: derives all 62 constituents, including amplitudes and the two-bulge pattern, from a distant moon.
patches.replace: derives hundreds of tidal constituents (389 in the full harmonic expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset), including amplitudes and the two-bulge pattern, from a distant moon.
patches.rationale: Doodson (1921) actually derived 389 harmonic constituents, not 62. The '62' is a practical subset used in many analyses but the attribution of exactly 62 to Doodson 1921 is imprecise. Correcting the claim to 'hundreds' with a parenthetical is more accurate and defensible.

patches.replace: produces all major tidal constituents (389 in the full Doodson expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset)
patches.rationale: Same precision issue as WIN-045. Doodson derived 389 constituents, not exactly 62. Correcting with parenthetical note.

patches.find: The functional form is wrong, not just the parameters.
patches.replace: The functional form is wrong, not just the parameters. The dome's own V9 coordinate system makes this borrowed dependence explicit: lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude_june + 23.44 — the globe's 23.44° obliquity appears as a direct numerical input for computing latitude from solar observations (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt, line 25). The dome cannot derive this constant from its own geometry; it must borrow it from the spherical model it claims to replace.
patches.rationale: The dome's V9 coordinate formula (lat_S = 90 - noon_altitude_june + 23.44) is smoking-gun evidence that the dome model treats the globe's obliquity as a known input constant. Adding this citation from the dome's own source file directly substantiates the claim that the dome borrows the globe's math.

patches.find: The westward drift of the Atlantic cell is well-documented by NOAA's continuous monitoring.
patches.replace: The westward drift of the Atlantic cell is well-documented by NOAA's continuous monitoring. The CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model (Finlay et al., 2020) quantifies the asymmetry: the South Atlantic (Brazilian) cell decays at roughly 160–200 nT/year while the East Indian Ocean cell decays at roughly 80–120 nT/year — a factor-of-two differential that MHD core dynamics reproduce from first principles but that the dome model, lacking any fluid core mechanism, cannot account for.
patches.rationale: ISS-263 and ISS-264 both flag that the evidence is thin: one citation to Terra-Nova et al. and a generic NOAA link, with no quantified decay rate asymmetry. Adding CHAOS-7 (the standard reference for current geomagnetic field modeling) with specific decay rate numbers makes the argument concrete and harder to dismiss. The CHAOS-7 DOI is 10.1186/s40623-020-01163-9 (Earth, Planets and Space, 2020).
